title: Prefixed Verbs & Aspect Pairs
description: Learn how Czech prefixes transform motion verbs into specific directional meanings: přijít/přijet (to arrive), odejít/odjet (to leave), vyjít/vyjet (to go out), dojít/dojet (to reach/arrive at). Master aspect pairs — imperfective (process/habitual) vs perfective (completed result) — and learn to choose the right aspect in context with direction adverbs.

exercises.prompt: What does the prefix 'při-' add to 'jít' (to go)?

data.question_en: What does the prefix 'při-' add to 'jít' (to go)?
data.explanation: 'Při-' = arrival, coming toward the speaker or a reference point. 'Přijít' = to arrive (on foot), 'přijet' = to arrive (by vehicle). Example: 'Zítra přijedu do Prahy.' = Tomorrow I will arrive in Prague (by vehicle). The prefix 'při-' always signals movement toward. Contrast with 'od-' (leaving/away).

exercises.prompt: Which is the perfective (completed) form of 'odcházet' (to be leaving, habitual)?

data.question_cz: Který tvar je dokonavý (výsledek) ke slovesu 'odcházet'?
data.question_en: Which is the perfective (completed) form of 'odcházet' (to be leaving, habitual)?
data.explanation: 'Odcházet' (imperfective — process of leaving, habitual) pairs with 'odejít' (perfective — completed act of leaving). Example imperfective: 'Každý den odcházím v šest.' = Every day I leave at six. Example perfective: 'Včera jsem odešel v šest.' = Yesterday I left at six (completed). The perfective focuses on the result; the imperfective on the process or habit.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the perfective form: 'Yesterday I ___ to school.' (on foot, completed)

data.explanation: 'Včera jsem šel do školy.' (masculine) / 'Včera jsem šla do školy.' (feminine) = Yesterday I went to school. 'Šel/šla' is the past tense of 'jít' — a perfective verb (one-time, completed). 'Včera' (yesterday) signals a completed past event, so we use the perfective 'jít' → past 'šel' (masc.) / 'šla' (fem.).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct prefixed verb: 'The train ___ to the station at 5 PM.' (arrive, by vehicle, completed)

data.explanation: 'Vlak přijel na nádraží v 17 hodin.' = The train arrived at the station at 5 PM. 'Přijel' = arrived (by vehicle, perfective past, 3rd person singular). The prefix 'při-' = arrival, and 'jet' → past 'přijel' (train is a vehicle). The perfective is used because the arrival is a completed event.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'He arrived home on foot at eight o'clock.'

data.source: He arrived home on foot at eight o'clock.
accepted_answers: On přišel domů v osm hodin.
data.grammar_note: 'Přišel domů v osm hodin.' = He arrived home on foot at eight o'clock. 'Přišel' = he arrived (perfective past of 'přijít' — arrival on foot, masculine). 'Domů' = home (direction adverb). 'V osm hodin' = at eight o'clock. The prefix 'při-' signals arrival, and 'jít' (on foot) → past 'přišel'. The perfective is used because the arrival is completed.

'Odjíždíme' = we are leaving (imperfective present of 'odjíždět' — departing by vehicle, 1st person plural). The prefix 'od-' = leaving/departing, and 'jezdit' → 'odjíždět' (by vehicle, habitual/process). 'Zítra ráno' = tomorrow morning. The imperfective 'odjíždět' here describes a planned future departure as a process. The perfective equivalent 'odjedeme' would emphasize completion.

exercises.prompt: Match each imperfective verb to its perfective partner (aspect pair).

data.prompt_en: Match each imperfective verb (process/habitual) to its perfective partner (completed result).
data.explanation: Czech verbs come in aspect pairs: imperfective (process, habit, ongoing) + perfective (completed result). Motion verbs form pairs by changing the prefix/stem: 'přicházet' (to be arriving, impf.) → 'přijít' (to arrive, pf.); 'odcházet' (to be leaving on foot, impf.) → 'odejít' (to leave on foot, pf.); 'odjíždět' (to be departing by vehicle, impf.) → 'odjet' (to depart by vehicle, pf.); 'přijíždět' (to be arriving by vehicle, impf.) → 'přijet' (to arrive by vehicle, pf.).

exercises.prompt: Match each prefixed motion verb to its English meaning.

data.prompt_en: Match each prefixed Czech motion verb to its English meaning.
data.explanation: Prefixes give motion verbs precise directional meanings: 'při-' = arrival toward, 'od-' = departure away, 'vy-' = going out/exit, 'do-' = reaching a destination. Combined with 'jít' (on foot) or 'jet' (by vehicle): 'přijít' = arrive on foot, 'odjet' = depart by vehicle, 'vyjít' = go out on foot, 'dojet' = reach by vehicle. The prefix + base verb together encode both direction and mode of travel.

exercises.answer_key: přijít→to arrive (on foot), odjet→to leave/depart (by vehicle), vyjít→to go out (on foot), dojet→to reach/arrive (by vehicle)

exercises.prompt: Find the mistake: wrong aspect (imperfective used where perfective is needed).

data.prompt_en: Find the mistake — the wrong verb aspect is used.
data.sentence_cz: Včera jsem odcházel z práce v pět.
data.correct_sentence_cz: Včera jsem odešel z práce v pět.
data.explanation: 'Včera' (yesterday) describes a specific completed event in the past. For a single completed action, use the perfective 'odejít' → past 'odešel' (masc.). 'Odcházel' is from 'odcházet' (imperfective — habitual or ongoing process), which would suit 'Každý den jsem odcházel v pět' (every day I used to leave at five). Correct: 'Včera jsem odešel z práce v pět.' = Yesterday I left work at five (completed).

exercises.answer_key: Včera jsem odešel z práce v pět.

exercises.prompt: Find the mistake: wrong aspect (perfective used where imperfective is needed for a habit).

data.prompt_en: Find the mistake — the wrong verb aspect is used for a habitual action.
data.sentence_cz: Každý den jsem odešel z domova v sedm.
data.correct_sentence_cz: Každý den jsem odcházel z domova v sedm.
data.explanation: 'Každý den' (every day) signals a habitual, repeated action in the past. For repeated/habitual actions, use the imperfective 'odcházet' → past 'odcházel' (masc.). 'Odešel' is from 'odejít' (perfective — single completed event), which would suit 'Včera jsem odešel v sedm' (yesterday I left at seven). Correct: 'Každý den jsem odcházel z domova v sedm.' = Every day I used to leave home at seven.

exercises.answer_key: Každý den jsem odcházel z domova v sedm.

exercises.prompt: Complete the navigation dialogue with the correct prefixed motion verb.

data.prompt_en: Complete the dialogue at a train station. Choose the correct form of 'přijet' (to arrive, by vehicle, perfective).

lines.speaker: Cestující
lines.text_en: When does the train to Brno arrive?
lines.explanation: Either 'přijede' (perfective future — will arrive) or 'přijíždí' (imperfective present — arrives habitually) is acceptable. 'Přijet' = to arrive by vehicle; 'přijíždět' = to be arriving by vehicle (imperfective). For a schedule, the imperfective 'přijíždí' is common (habitual per schedule); for a specific upcoming train, 'přijede' (perfective future) is used.

lines.speaker: Pokladní
lines.text_en: The train to Brno arrives at 14:30 on platform 3.
lines.explanation: 'Přijíždí' (imperfective) is natural for a scheduled, recurring arrival. 'Přijede' (perfective) works for a specific one-time arrival. Both are grammatically correct; 'přijíždí' is slightly more natural for schedules. 'Na nástupišti 3' = on platform 3.
lines.text: Vlak do Brna ___ v 14:30 na nástupišti 3.

data.explanation: This dialogue practices prefixed motion verbs in a real navigation context. 'Při-' = arrival, combined with 'jet' (by vehicle). At a train station, you often hear both aspects: imperfective for regular schedules ('přijíždí v 14:30' = arrives at 14:30 per schedule) and perfective for a specific upcoming arrival ('přijede v 14:30' = will arrive at 14:30 today).
data.scenario: A traveler asks when the train to Brno arrives. The clerk responds.

exercises.answer_key: Kdy přijíždí vlak do Brna? — Vlak do Brna přijíždí v 14:30 na nástupišti 3.

exercises.prompt: Arrange the words to form: 'We arrived at the station on foot.'

data.explanation: 'My přišli na nádraží pěšky.' = We arrived at the station on foot. 'Přišli' = we arrived (perfective past of 'přijít' — arrival on foot, 1st person plural, masculine/animate). 'Na nádraží' = at/to the station (accusative with 'na' for destination). 'Pěšky' = on foot. The prefix 'při-' signals arrival. Perfective is used because the arrival is completed.
